vulnerability.title: Modify the System Login Banner
vulnerability.description: To configure the system login banner edit /etc/issue. Replace the default text with a message compliant with the local site policy or a legal disclaimer. The DoD required text is either: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (PM), law enforcement (LE), and counterintelligence (CI) investigations. -At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS. -Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, interception, and search, and may be disclosed or used for any USG-authorized purpose. -This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests -- not for your personal benefit or privacy. -Notwithstanding the above, using this IS does not constitute consent to PM, LE or CI investigative searching or monitoring of the content of privileged communications, or work product, related to personal representation or services by attorneys, psychotherapists, or clergy, and their assistants. Such communications and work product are private and confidential. See User Agreement for details. OR: I've read & consent to terms in IS user agreem't.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then login_banner_text='' # Multiple regexes transform the banner regex into a usable banner # 0 - Remove anchors around the banner text login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^\^\(.*\)\$$/\1/g') # 1 - Keep only the first banners if there are multiple # (dod_banners contains the long and short banner) login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^(\(.*\.\)|.*)$/\1/g') # 2 - Add spaces ' '. (Transforms regex for "space or newline" into a " ") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/\[\\s\\n\]+/ /g') # 3 - Adds newlines. (Transforms "(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\n)+)" into "\n") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\\\n)+)/\n/g') # 4 - Remove any

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, bootloader, or critical services. The change is reversible and does not risk loss of SSH or console access.

execution_details.stdout: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel

llm_verdict.message:
Implemented the required remediation for the **banner_etc_issue** finding:

* Replaced the contents of **/etc/issue** with the full DoD‑required login banner text.  
* Ensured file ownership is `root:root` and permissions are `0644`.  
* Restored the proper SELinux context using `restorecon`.

The system now displays the correct login banner on console logins, satisfying the OpenSCAP rule `banner_etc_issue`.

review_feedback: The plan correctly updates /etc/issue permissions and SELinux context, but the banner text provided is incomplete and likely will not satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Additionally, there is no verification step to confirm that the file contains the exact required disclaimer after writing, and no backup of the original file is made.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will replace /etc/issue with the required banner and thus satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. Ensure the full DoD disclaimer text is used and preserve correct ownership and SELinux context.
concerns: The provided banner text appears truncated and may not match the exact required DoD disclaimer.
File SELinux context is not addressed; writing the file may result in an incorrect context and cause the banner not to be displayed.
Remote SSH logins use /etc/issue.net; if the policy requires it, only editing /etc/issue may be insufficient.
suggested_improvements: Copy the complete DoD banner verbatim as defined in the vulnerability description.
After writing, verify and, if needed, restore the SELinux context (e.g., restorecon -v /etc/issue).
Set file ownership to root:root and confirm mode 0644.
Optionally update /etc/issue.net or SSHBanner setting if the organization requires a banner for SSH sessions.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly updates /etc/issue permissions and SELinux context, but the banner text provided is incomplete and likely will not satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Additionally, there is no verification step to confirm that the file contains the exact required disclaimer after writing, and no backup of the original file is made.
concerns: Banner text appears truncated and may not match the full DoD disclaimer required by the rule.
Only /etc/issue is modified; if the organization also requires an SSH banner, /etc/issue.net or the SSHBanner setting may need updating.
No verification step to ensure the written content exactly matches the required text.
No backup of the original /etc/issue before overwriting.
suggested_improvements: Copy the complete DoD disclaimer verbatim, preserving line breaks and spacing, as defined in the vulnerability description.
Create a backup of /etc/issue (e.g., cp /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak) before overwriting.
Add a verification step after writing, such as diff or grep, to confirm the content matches the expected banner.
Optionally update /etc/issue.net or configure the SSHBanner directive if an SSH login banner is also required by policy.
Consider using restorecon -v /etc/issue for logging the SELinux context restoration.
